utterances: I'm Ira Flatow, and this is TALK OF THE NATION: SCIENCE FRIDAY from NPR News.

You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION: SCIENCE FRIDAY.
I'm Ira Flatow, and we're talking this hour about science books.
And joining us now is Heather Malcomson, editor of Science Books and Films for the American Association for the Advancement of Science.
Welcome to the program, Heather.
Give us your top five, or as many as you'd like to give us.
Well, sure, sure.
Well, as editor of Science Books and Films I'm lucky enough to get to look through hundreds of children's science books each year, so it's really a great job.
And I've broken my picks up into categories by age.
I have a couple for very young children, say in grades about K to three, and then a couple for your middle school-aged children.
So I'd have to say that one of my favorites books this year is a book called "An Egg is Quiet.
And I think when children really start to explore nature, they kind of become fascinated by eggs.
It might be one of the first things they come across, you know, out in their backyard or at the zoo or the playground and...
Or the kitchen.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Exactly.
And this book, the highlight of it is really the truly amazing and detailed drawings that Sylvia Long has done.
But beside from the drawings, there's just a great text, and it's really almost lyrical, but it's very educational.
And children will learn all kinds of cool and interesting things about eggs.

From why there are different shapes - like the seabird egg, which is actually pointed on one end so that when it rolls around, it rolls around in these safe little circles instead of rolling off of a cliff; or to why they're different colors, usually for camouflage; to different textures, like amphibian eggs are kind of gooey so that they stay moist and safe.
So the author and illustrator just do a great job of bringing eggs to life for kids, and I don't think kids will really ever look at an egg the same after seeing this book.
What's the name again?
Because now people are interested, they're going to write it down.
Sure, it's called "An Egg Is Quiet.
Mm-hmm.
OK.
Right, and I guess my next pick here is a subject that's very exciting to young kids, and that's dinosaurs.
And there's a great book called "Boy, Were We Wrong About Dinosaurs.
And just the title I think will make kids want to pick it up and read it.
Mm-hmm.
And it really corrects all kinds of misconceptions and misinformation that kids may have picked up on over the years related to dinosaurs.
And it's full of just really cool illustrations, which are very important to children's science books.
And the tone the author takes is just kind of humorous.

One of the things I learned in it, for example, the author tells how researchers, when they were unearthing these remains of an Iguanodon dinosaur, they placed these rhino-like horn bones on the nose of the Iguanodon.
But years and years later, when the full skeletal fossil was unearthed, they actually realized that these horn-like bones belong on the front limbs of the dinosaur.
So, there's all kinds of neat stories in the book like that.
That reminds me of the Brontosaurus at the Museum of Natural History, where the head was wrong.
It was...
Right, right.
And that's just to kids, I think that really catches their attention and just makes them think about, you know, about the book and dinosaurs in general.
It's just a cool book.
Does it mention that "The Flintstones" is not real?
(Soundbite of laughter)
There weren't dinosaurs living with people?
No, it doesn't.
I don't think it touches on that subject - maybe in the sequel.
I'm not sure.
(Soundbite of laughter)
And for older kids, what have you got for older kids?
For older kids I've got two great books.
One is called "Team Moon: How 400,000 People Landed Apollo 11 on the Moon.
And this book takes readers kind of behind the scenes of the mission that first placed us on the moon.

And it tells the story of all the non-astronauts that made the mission possible, from the seamstress who sewed the high-tech uniforms to the engineers who created all those amazing heat shields for reentry.
And it just really tells the stories that, you know, you usually don't hear about.
I want to read that one.
Yeah, it's really great.
Am I allowed to read that?
Certainly.
I can send a copy over for you.
(Soundbite of laughter)
And it's got great photographs in it, which are important for children's science books, you know, because they really - that's just a whole another layer to the book.
Are there any trends in kids' science books this year?
I mean do you see more of the other?
Well...
Any environmental books?
We're talking about global warming.
Are there any books like that perhaps about the environment, or...
Abdalrazag says often a boat like this will just be a makeshift raft, plastic tubes glued to wood.

And they might touch - you know, they have chapters that kind of touch on that, but it - and it depends on the age level, too.
Yeah.
Because for very young usually that's a topic that's not really touched on.
Yeah.
Animal books are always popular.
Oh, yes, they are.
(Unintelligible) animal books for us?
I do.
Actually, you read my mind.
My last - the last book on my list is called "ER Vets: Life in an Animal Emergency Room.
And, you know, what child hasn't said at some point in their life, you know, I want to be a veterinarian.
You know, I know I did.
So this book is really for those kids who, you know, dream of being a veterinarian and helping out animals.
And it just goes behind the scenes again to an animal hospital in Colorado.
And it has all these great pictures.
And it shows, you know, not just cats and dogs, but there's a great story of a snake named Lucy who gets some treatment and a horse.
And it's just a really neat book, and I don't think it'll spend much time on the shelf.
I think kids will really be picking up that one a lot.
